change_description: UI styling or layout change, color, font, spacing, CSS
raw_feedback: UI-only styling changes are always faster than graph score suggests. The dependency graph connects UI to backend but those connections are not actually touched for visual changes.
principle: Surface area principle: import graph overstates coupling for pure visual changes

change_description: Adding a new UI component or widget
raw_feedback: Adding a contained UI component is well-understood work. It rarely requires backend changes and the effort is predictable.
principle: Familiarity principle: UI component patterns are repetitive and well-understood

change_description: UI change that requires new data from the backend or API
raw_feedback: When UI changes need new backend data, the effort is regularly underestimated because it requires coordination across the stack.
principle: Integration depth principle: cross-layer changes multiply effort

change_description: Adding a new API endpoint
raw_feedback: Adding a new API endpoint is well-scoped work when the data model already exists. Estimate is usually accurate.
principle: Familiarity principle: new endpoints follow established patterns

change_description: Modifying an existing API contract, changing request or response shape
raw_feedback: Changing existing API contracts is consistently underestimated because all consumers must be found, updated, and tested.
principle: Risk principle: breaking changes propagate to all consumers

change_description: Authentication, authorization, login, OAuth, SSO, session, permissions
raw_feedback: Auth changes are almost always underestimated. They touch security-critical paths, require edge case handling, and carry high production risk.
principle: Risk principle: auth is a critical system — bugs have severe consequences

change_description: Performance optimization, speed improvement, latency reduction, memory usage
raw_feedback: Performance tasks are highly uncertain. The root cause is often unknown upfront, profiling reveals surprises, and optimizations frequently require architectural changes.
principle: Unknown principle: vague performance goals = high uncertainty multiplier

change_description: Database migration, schema change, adding or removing columns or tables
raw_feedback: Database migrations are consistently underestimated. Data integrity risks, rollback planning, and production deployment concerns add significant overhead beyond the code change itself.
principle: Risk principle: data integrity is irreversible — migrations require extra caution

change_description: Adding a database index or query optimization
raw_feedback: Adding indexes is usually safe and fast. The change is contained, the pattern is well-understood, and rollback is straightforward.
principle: Surface area principle: index additions are isolated and reversible

change_description: Configuration change, environment variable, feature flag, settings file
raw_feedback: Configuration changes are usually fast. They are contained, well-understood, and rarely require touching application logic.
principle: Surface area principle: config changes are isolated from business logic

change_description: Environment variable, secret management, credentials, API keys
raw_feedback: Secret and credential management changes are faster to code but carry production deployment risk that is often not accounted for in estimates.
principle: Risk principle: production secrets require coordinated deployment

change_description: Adding unit tests, writing test cases
raw_feedback: Writing unit tests is well-understood and predictable work. Effort is usually slightly overestimated.
principle: Familiarity principle: test patterns are repetitive and well-scoped

change_description: Adding integration tests, end-to-end tests
raw_feedback: Integration tests are harder than unit tests due to environment setup, flakiness, and dependency management. Estimates are usually accurate or slightly low.
principle: Integration depth principle: integration tests expose real system complexity

change_description: Refactoring, restructuring, cleaning up code without changing behavior
raw_feedback: Refactoring is regularly underestimated. It pulls in more files than expected via import chains, and verifying no behavioral change requires thorough testing.
principle: Amplification principle: refactoring spreads through import graphs unpredictably

change_description: Renaming modules, reorganizing folder structure, moving files
raw_feedback: Module renaming and reorganization is consistently underestimated because every import across the codebase must be found and updated.
principle: Propagation principle: renames propagate through all import references

change_description: Integrating a third-party API, external service, SDK, or library
raw_feedback: Third-party integrations always take longer than estimated. API exploration, undocumented edge cases, rate limits, authentication flows, and error handling all add hidden complexity.
principle: Unknown principle: external APIs introduce unknowns that cannot be anticipated

change_description: Bug fix in an isolated or well-understood module
raw_feedback: Isolated bug fixes in well-understood modules are usually faster than estimated. The scope is clear and the pattern is known.
principle: Familiarity principle: known systems with clear bugs are fast to fix

change_description: Bug fix where root cause is unknown, intermittent bug, hard to reproduce
raw_feedback: Bugs with unknown root causes are massively underestimated. Investigation time alone can exceed the fix time by 5x.
principle: Unknown principle: unknown root cause = investigation overhead dominates

change_description: Feature touching multiple layers — frontend, backend, database together
raw_feedback: Cross-layer features are the most commonly underestimated category. Coordination overhead, integration testing, and dependency sequencing all add time beyond individual layer estimates.
principle: Integration depth principle: cross-layer work multiplies coordination overhead

change_description: Adding logging, monitoring, metrics, alerting, observability
raw_feedback: Logging and monitoring additions are usually fast and lower than graph score suggests because they add to modules without changing their logic.
principle: Surface area principle: observability additions are additive, not disruptive
